coredata.dc:title: Impressive nonlinear optical response of π-conjugated, photoluminescent soft nanoparticles in polymer matrix
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Dandan
Zhang, Jie
Deng, Mingzhi
Liu, Xiaoqian
Liu, Ziyao
Yin, Keyang
Li, Hongguang
coredata.dc:description: Nonlinear optical (NLO) materials are useful for applications varying from modulation of optical signals to cancer therapy. Besides inorganic crystals and organic molecules with defined structures, carbonized products have emerged as a new generation of materials showing good NLO properties. Due to the small sizes, the NLO performance of such materials mainly originates from nonlinear absorption. Obtaining a product with a high content of π-conjugated moiety is the key to improving the NLO performance. By using aldehydes bearing π units as the precursors, in this work we synthesized three polymeric nanoparticles (PNPs) through one-step pyrolysis, which also have large contents of π-conjugated structures. It was found that a larger size of π unit in the precursor led to a higher content as well as the size of the π-conjugated structure, which induced a bathochromic shift in the emission and an improved performance in the NLO response. When embedded in polymethylmethacrylate films, the products showed reverse saturable absorption (RSA) behavior with β up to 4.20 × 10−6 m W−1 and optical limit threshold down to 0.0012 J cm−2, which are comparable to those of metal-doped counterparts. This work gives deep insight on the structure-property relationship of the pyrolyzed products from π-conjugated small molecules, which is also useful for the preparation of task-specific carbon dots (CDs) or PNPs in future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nonlinear optical
π-conjugated
Polymeric
Nanoparticles
Reverse saturable absorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties of the ground and excited states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d) Typical TEM images of the PNPs, prepared from dispersions in toluene. For PNPs-2, two images with different magnifications are given. (e) Statistics of the sizes of the PNPs. The arow is a guide for the eyes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Magnified TEM image on a typical particle from PNPs-1. b) AFM image of PNPs-2. c) Height profiles along the lines for two typical particles marked in image b. d) Illustration of a deformed particle of PNPs-2 on solid substrate (TEM grid or silica wafer). Yellow: the sp3-hybridized carbon. Grey: The π-conjugated units. Red: the carbonyl groups. e) Statistics of the sizes of the PNPs-2 obtained by different imaging techniques as indicated. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–c) AFM images of the PNPs. Samples were prepared from dispersions in CH2Cl2. Bottom of each image is the height profile of the marked particle. The magnification of the three images is the same, with the scale bar (given in image a) to be 200 nm. (d–f) Comparison of the sizes of the three PNPs obtained from different imaging studies. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XRD patterns (a), Raman spectra (b), XPS survey (c) and statistics of each component (d) of C1s peaks, FTIR spectra (e), 1H NMR (f) and 13C NMR (g) and for the three PNPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–c) Normalized UV–vis absorption of the PNPs (0.3 mg mL−1), which is in comparison with data from the precursors (2.0 mg mL−1). Samples were prepared in CH2Cl2. (d) Band gaps of the three PNPs calculated from their absorption band edges. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Steady-state fluorescence spectra at four concentrations (a–c) and the two-dimensional spectra at the optimized concentrations (d–f) of PNPs-1 (a, d), PNPs-2 (b, e) and PNPs-3 (c, f). The λ ex was varied from 340 nm to 600 nm with a step of 10 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photos of the PMMA-based composite films containing surfactant (Tw 20) and PNPs. The weight ratio of PNPs, Tw 20 and PMMA is ∼1:10:1000. Taken under room light (top) and 365 nm UV irradiation (bottom). (b) Transmittance of the composite films. The dashed line is a guide for the eyes. (c) Normalized transmittance of the composite films obtained from open aperture Z-scan at ∼3.0 μJ. (d) Statistics of the β values of the composite films obtained at different laser energies as indicated. (e) Optical limiting curve of the composite film containing PNPs-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Illustration of the preparation of the three PNPs from corresponding precursors.
Illustration of the structures of the three PNPs. Middle: Overview. Left: Cross-sectional view. Right: Magnification on part of the surface.
Parameters of the three PL PNPs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: From TEM observations (samples were dispersed in toluene).

head.ce:title: Impressive nonlinear optical response of π-conjugated, photoluminescent soft nanoparticles in polymer matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Novel Semiconductors, State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, Shandong, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Dandan Lu and Jie Zhang means that these authors contribute equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nonlinear optical (NLO) materials are useful for applications varying from modulation of optical signals to cancer therapy. Besides inorganic crystals and organic molecules with defined structures, carbonized products have emerged as a new generation of materials showing good NLO properties. Due to the small sizes, the NLO performance of such materials mainly originates from nonlinear absorption. Obtaining a product with a high content of π-conjugated moiety is the key to improving the NLO performance. By using aldehydes bearing π units as the precursors, in this work we synthesized three polymeric nanoparticles (PNPs) through one-step pyrolysis, which also have large contents of π-conjugated structures. It was found that a larger size of π unit in the precursor led to a higher content as well as the size of the π-conjugated structure, which induced a bathochromic shift in the emission and an improved performance in the NLO response. When embedded in polymethylmethacrylate films, the products showed reverse saturable absorption (RSA) behavior with β up to 4.20 × 10−6 m W−1 and optical limit threshold down to 0.0012 J cm−2, which are comparable to those of metal-doped counterparts. This work gives deep insight on the structure-property relationship of the pyrolyzed products from π-conjugated small molecules, which is also useful for the preparation of task-specific carbon dots (CDs) or PNPs in future.

TOC GraphicPyrolysis of π-conjugated aldehydes yielded soft polymeric nanoparticles (PNPs). A larger size of π-conjugated moiety in the precursor yielded PNPs with longer emission wavelength and better nonlinear optical response.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In 1961, Franken et al. observed second-harmonic generation (SHG) in quartz [1]. Since then, nonlinear optical (NLO) phenomenon has attracted much attention and been developed into one of the key branches of modern optics. Materials with NLO properties have been widely used in high-speed optical switching and modulation, photodetector, three-dimensional micro- and nanofabrication, coherent optical communication and optical limiting [2–13]. Metal-containing inorganic materials are the first generation of NLO materials, with typical examples including KH2PO4 (KDP) [14], KTiOPO4 (KTP) [15], LiB3O5 (LBO) [16], β-BaB2O4 (BBO) [17,18], and AgGaS2 (AGS) [19]. In the mid of 1980s, organic dyes appeared as the second generation of NLO materials [20]. Compared to inorganic materials, organic ones exhibit large and fast nonlinearities as well as high optical damage thresholds [21–28]. Features of organic molecules in favor of getting a high NLO performance have been recognized [29–34], and famous candidates have been reported, including cyanine dyes [35], derivatives of stilbene [32,33], porphyrin [36,37] and phthalocyanine [38–40].

Besides inorganic crystals and organic molecules, some nanomaterials also exhibit pronounced NLO response [41–47]. Typical examples are carbon dots (CDs), which are a class of photoluminescent (PL) carbon nanomaterials [48]. They could be facilely obtained from etching of bulk carbon materials, or by pyrolysis of various organic molecules [49]. Unlike inorganic crystals and organic molecules which have explicit composition or molecular structure, CDs are actually a mixture. Their compositions are highly dependent on the raw materials used and the handling method during preparation. This feature makes it difficult to deep understand the structure-property relationship in their NLO response, although accumulated evidences have led to some conclusions such as to achieving a good NLO performance, doping of heteroatoms is necessary [47].

Adopting the same strategy to prepare CDs, in this work we prepared three types of PL carbon nanomaterials by pyrolysis of three aldehyde-functionalized, π-conjugated precursors. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , after reflux in bromobenzene, the samples were subjected to purification by gel permeation chromatography (GPC). The sizes of the products are tens of nanometers, which already exceed the definition commonly adopted for CDs. They are soft and undergo large shape deformation when deposited onto solid substates. We then classified them into soft, polymeric nanoparticles (PNPs). Investigations showed that all the three PNPs exhibit third-order NLO response with reverse saturable absorption behavior. The performance scales with the extent and the size of π-conjugated moieties inside the PNPs, where PNPs from 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde (PA, PNPs-3) took the crown. The nonlinear optical coefficient (β) reaches 4.2 × 10−6 m W−1, which is among the highest values achieved for carbon nanomaterials [46,47].

Bromobenzene (BB, analytical grade), fluorene-2-carboxaldehyde (FA, 98 %), 2-naphthaldehyde (NA, 98 %) and 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde (PA, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA, average Mw: ∼350,000) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Didodecyldimethylammonium bromide (DDAB, 98 %), polyethylene-polypropylene glycol (F127, average Mn: ∼12,000), tween 20 (Tw 20), cholesterol (CHT, 99 %) were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol, toluene, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), and n-hexane were obtained from local supplier with the quality of analytical grade. All chemicals were used without further purification. Glasswares, including 60 mm glass petri dishes were also obtained from local supplier.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a spectrometer (Tensor II) using KBr pellets. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of PNPs were recorded on a JEM2100 instrument operating at 200 kV. For sample preparation, a drop of sample solution (20 μL, 1 mg mL−1 in toluene) was placed on a holey-carbon coated copper grid (300 meshes) and dried by an infrared lamp for ∼20 min. For atomic force microscope (AFM) observations, 10 μL sample solution was dropped on a silica wafer and then dried with infrared lamp for ∼20 min before observation in a tapping mode operating with Bioscope Resolve Atomic Force Microscopy (Bruker) at a scan frequency of 0.994 Hz. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source (1486.71 eV). Raman spectra were obtained from a confocal microscopic Raman spectrometer (Lab Ram HR Evolution). UV–vis measurements of the PNPs solution (dissolved in CH2Cl2) were carried out on a UV–vis–NIR spectrometer (cary 5000). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by a spectrofluorometer (FluoroMax-4). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) and fluorescence lifetime (τ) were measured with a spectrofluorometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments LTD). For measurement of Φ, the PNPs were dissolved in CH2Cl2 for PNPs of optical measurements. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a BrukerAvance 400 spectrometer (Bruker Germany) using CDCl3 as a solvent.

PNPs were prepared by a pyrolysis method established by us recently to obtain CDs [46,47]. In a typical synthesis, desired amount of each precursor (aldehyde, 1.0 mol L−1) was dissolved in 50 mL BB in a three-necked flask with a volume of 100 mL. The mixture was then refluxed for 48 h under magnetic stirring, followed by being naturally cooled to room temperature. Three molecules were tested as precursors, including FA, NA and PA. After reflux, BB was removed under reduced pressure. The raw materials were redissolved in toluene and purified by gel permeation chromatography (Bio-Beads S-X3 Beads). Finally, toluene was removed under reduced pressure and the crude product was dried in vacuum for further characterizations. The three purified PNPs from FA, NA and PA were named PNPs-1, PNPs-2 and PNPs-3, respectively.

The PNPs/PMMA composite films were prepared by a simple volatilization method. In a typical experiment, 85.7 mg PMMA was added to 2 mL of CH2Cl2. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min to aid the total dissolution of PMMA. To this CH2Cl2 solution of PMMA was added 0.86 mg PNPs and 0.086 mg surfactant which were pre-dissolved in 1 mL CH2Cl2. The mixed solution was sonicated for 10 min before being poured into a glass Petri dish with a diameter of 60 mm. The sample was then kept at room temperature for 24 h for solvent evaporation. Substances from a variety of categories were tested for dispersing CNPs, including three surfactants (DDAB, F127, Tw 20) and CHT.

Third-order NLO absorption of the composite films was evaluated using the open-aperture Z-scan technique (Beamtech Optronics Co., Ltd.). A Q-switched Nd: YAG 532 nm laser with a pulse width of 5 ns and a repetition rate of 10 Hz was used as the light source. The laser pulses were focused onto the film using a converging lens and the sample was moved from -z to + z, where z = 0 represents the focal point and the data were collected using a dual-channel power meter that was interfaced with a computer using LabVIEW software. The nonlinear absorption coefficient is calculated from the intensity dependent transmittance (T), which was measured as a function of z. The pulse repetition rate was 10 Hz and the beam waist at focus was calculated to be 18 μm. The Rayleigh range was calculated to be 1912.3 μm. Each measurement was performed at least twice to ensure the reproducibility of the data.

The so-obtained pyrolyzed products were re-dispersed in toluene and subjected to transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations. Typical TEM images are given in Fig. 1 a–d. The particles are well dispersed without severe aggregation. Statistics showed that the majority of the particles have sizes over 10 nm but less than 200 nm (Fig. 1e). From PNPs-1 to PNPs-3, the size distribution slightly increased to higher values. The polydispersity of PNPs-2 is heavier compared to PNPs-1 and PNPs-3, with presence of particles over 200 nm.

For a solid particle with perfect spherical shape, normally the electron density in the center is heavier than that in the edge under TEM. However, it is not the case for the three PNPs. From Fig. 1a–d, for most particles the electron density in the center and the edge does not differ much. This feature is better seen from magnified images of single particles, as presented in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1, respectively, indicating that the particle is actually flat on the TEM grid. This conclusion gains further proof from atomic force microscopy (AFM) observations. Taking PNPs-2 for example, height analysis on two typical particles showed that the height is only about one tenth that of the width (Fig. 2b and c). Considering that both TEM and AFM observations were performed on the dried samples, it is plausible to conclude that the particles collapsed on TEM grid or silica wafer, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. In other words, the particles underwent significant shape deformation on solid substrates during drying, which meant that they are actually soft in nature. Further analysis showed that under AFM, the polydispersity became narrower compared to that under TEM (Fig. 2e). This observation indicates that the sample preparation (see experimental section) and type of solid substrate, which is different in TEM and AFM, could influence the shrinkage and shape deformation of the particles, presumably through kinetic aspect.

Besides toluene, the PNPs could also be dispersed in other nonpolar organic solvents. Their morphologies under AFM when engineered from CH2Cl2 were also investigated. From the results summarized in Fig. 3 a–c, the particles underwent great shape deformation on silicon wafer with the height significantly smaller than the width, which is fully consistent with the phenomenon using toluene as a solvent. Comparison between Figs. 3b and 2b showed that the particles appear quite different, which could be ascribed to the varied kinetics of solvent evaporation. Of course, the possibility of different swelling behavior of the particles in toluene and CH2Cl2 could not be excluded at the moment. Despite of this uncertainty, the high tendency of shape deformation and sensitivity toward solvent confirmed that the polymeric character of the particles. Consistent with what we have observed using toluene as a solvent, the polydispersity of the PNPs under AFM became smaller compared to that under TEM (Fig. 3d–f).

The structures of the PNPs were fully characterized by a variety of techniques, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. The results are summarized in Fig. 4 . From XRD (Fig. 4a), a broad diffraction ranging from 20 to 40° was observed for each sample, which indicated that the PNPs are amorphous. This observation is consistent with the results from TEM where no lattice fringes could be found inside the PNPs, which indicated that the three precursors only underwent gentle pyrolysis under current experimental condition. For PNPs-3, two peaks corresponding to a distance of 0.64 nm and 0.32 nm, respectively, were detected on the broad diffraction, which is indicative of its slightly higher internal order compared to the other two samples. We speculate that the 0.32 nm diffraction peak corresponds to a stacked graphene-like interlayer spacing, specifically referring to the parallel distance between π-π structures in polymer nanoparticles formed during the carbonization process of PA. This hypothesis is supported by both Raman spectra results and the structural depiction presented in Scheme 2 . PNPs-3 contains more π-conjugated structures [50]. At 13.8° corresponds to 0.64 nm, which also indicates π-π interlayer spacing in the polymeric nanoparticles, but this spacing is significantly increased compared to the bulk graphene (0.344 nm). The large expansion is attributed to the oxidation-induced incorporation of oxygen-containing groups into the interlayer [51–53]. In Raman spectra (Fig. 4b), pronounced G peaks were detected for the three PNPs together with weak D peaks, indicating that the PNPs are dominated with sp2 carbons. The contents of the sp2 carbons were further quantified by XPS. From the deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s spectra, one can see that the content of the sp2 carbon increases from PNPs-1 to PNPs-3 (Fig. 4c and d), indicating that a larger π-conjugated moiety in the precursor facilitates the formation of π-conjugated product.

Although one-step pyrolysis seems straightforward from a viewpoint of experimental operation, the mechanism behind might be complicated. During pyrolysis, condensation of organic functional groups will occur, which is accompanied with the carbonization of other parts of the molecule. The extent of the condensation and carbonization would rely on the property of the precursor, including its chemical and thermal stability. Changes of the three precursors were tracked by FTIR and NMR. From FTIR (Fig. 4e), a strong vibration of C=O at 1740-1690 cm−1 was detected for PNPs-1 [54], which greatly weakened for PNPs-2 and almost vanished for PNPs-3. Meanwhile, peaks in the range of 2925–2853 cm−1, which are assigned to the vibrations from the methylene groups become stronger from PNPs-1 to PNPs-3. Results from NMR are consistent with FTIR. As seen from Fig. 4f and g, signals from the methylene groups, which locate at 0.8–1.5 ppm in 1H NMR and below 50 ppm in 13C NMR, become more pronounced from PNPs-1 to PNPs-3. The results from FTIR and NMR showed that the majority of the carbonyl groups survived during pyrolysis for PNPs-1, while for PNPs-3, they were largely lost due to a heavier pyrolysis.

We realized that signals for the sp2 carbons and the hydrogens on them, which locate at 119–138 ppm in 13C NMR and 6.5–8.8 ppm in 1H NMR, respectively, weakened from PNPs-1 to PNPs-3, which is opposite to the trend obtained from XPS. This inconsistency could be caused by the difference between these two techniques. It is known that for solution NMR, groups well solvated would give much stronger signals compared to the solidified part, such as the center of the PNPs in current case. While XPS, which was carried out on solvent-free samples, can reach a much deeper length of the sample. As the PNPs are soft with a height typically less than 15 nm seen from AFM images, results of XPS could approximately represent the composition of the PNPs in a whole. The fact that the sp3 carbons (as well as the hydrogen atoms on them) are more visible by NMR indicates that they are mainly distributed on the surfaces of the PNPs. This could also explain their strong signals in FTIR spectra, a technique to preferentially detect surface groups with a more freedom instead of those embedded inside a particle. Models of the three PNPs, with illustration both inside and on surfaces, are given in Scheme 2.

It should be noted that besides the signals emphasized, others have also been observed. In FTIR, signals from C=C were detected in a range of 1603–1492 cm−1. Peaks located at 3440 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of –OH, C=C and respectively. In 1H NMR, peaks in the range of 4.7–6.0 ppm are attributed to the active H from –OH groups. The rich surface groups are characteristics for this type of material, including CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the ground and excited states
ce:section.ce:para: The electronic structures in the ground state of the PNPs in solution were explored by UV–vis measurements. The three PNPs showed obviously different absorption compared to the precursors (Fig. 5 a–c), indicating that structural changes successfully occurred during the pyrolysis. PNPs-1 exhibited two strong, adjacent peaks centered at ∼304 nm and ∼312 nm (Fig. 5a, indicated by the arrows), respectively. Combined the results from FTIR, these peaks could be assigned to the n-π* transition related to the C=O. For PNPs-2, no sharp peak could be detected (Fig. 5b). A shoulder peak and a small bulge were observed at ∼244 and 288 nm (indicated by the arrow), which could be assigned to the π-π* transition from the sp2 carbons [55,56]. These peaks became much more pronounced for PNPs-3 (Fig. 5c) caused by the improved content of the sp2 carbon, which is fully consistent with the results from XPS. Besides, two sharp peaks centered at ∼280 nm and ∼350 nm were observed, which were accompanied with three shoulder peaks (indicated by the arrows). The rich absorption of PNPs-3 is indicative of its complicated structure. At the same concentration, PA shows the strongest absorption in visible region (Fig. 5a–c, Fig. S2). After pyrolysis, PNPs-3 showed slightly lower absorption than PNPs-2 below 520 nm. Above 520 nm, however, its absorption became the strongest (Fig. S3), which yielded a lowest band energy (1.91 eV, Fig. 5d) among the three PNPs.

Properties in the excited state were probed by steady-state and time-resolved measurements. Emission as functions of concentration and excited wavelength (λ ex) is summarized in Fig. 6 a–c. In all the tested concentrations of the three PNPs, the emission gradually shifted toward longer wavelengths with increasing λ ex. The emission is quite broad, and the width at half height is easily over 100 nm. Such excitation-dependent, broad emission implies the existence of multiple emissive centers, a feature commonly found for most CDs. This means that although the PNPs presented here are much larger than traditional CDs, they still have similar PL properties with CDs. Variation of the PL intensity with concentration is dependent on the type of the PNPs. For PNPs-1, the highest intensity occurred at 2.0 mg mL−1 (Fig. 6a). At concentrations below and above, the intensity decreased greatly. For PNPs-2, the PL intensity is already considerably high at 0.5 mg mL−1 (Fig. 6b). The highest intensity occurred at 1.0 mg mL−1, with however only slightly higher intensity compared to that of 0.5 mg mL−1. PNPs-3 exhibited the highest PL intensity among the three PNPs, with the maximum also occurring at 2.0 mg mL−1 (Fig. 6c). For all the PNPs, the emission underwent bathochromic shift with increasing concentration.

The sample at the optimized concentration for each PNPs were further investigated by two-dimensional measurements. As seen from Fig. 6d–f, from PNPs-1 to PNPs-3 the maximum photoluminescence continuously shifted to longer wavelengths, which is 530 nm, 598 nm and 675 nm, respectively. Correspondingly, the λ ex at the maximum photoluminescence shifted from ∼410 nm for PNPs-1 to ∼540 nm for PNPs-3. The bathochromic shift indicated that the π-conjugated moieties became larger from PNPs-1 to PNPs-3, resulting in a lower energy needed for excitation. This conclusion is fully consistent with the results from UV–vis measurements.

Time-resolved measurements showed that the decay could be handled with a three-exponential fit (Fig. S4), which gave averaged lifetime (<τ>) below 5 ns (Table 1 ), indicating that the emission is fluorescence in nature. Due to the low absorption of PNPs-2 at the strongest emission, this sample gained the highest fluorescence quantum yield (Φ, 6.09 %). In comparison, PNPs-3 showed a slightly lower Φ of 3.42 % due to its strong absorption. The Φ of PNPs-1 is comparable with that of PNPs-3, which is 3.46 %.

The conjugated nature of the PNPs makes them ideal candidates for optoelectronic devices. The NLO response of the PNPs, which were embedded in the matrix of polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA), were investigated by open-aperture Z-scan technique using a 532 nm laser with a pulse width of 5 ns and a repetition rate of 10 Hz. As the π-conjugated structure of the PNPs is different from that of PMMA, phase separation that could be distinguished by naked eyes will inevitably occur, especially for PNPs-3 (Fig. S5). Surfactants were then used to suppress this macroscopic phase separation. After careful comparison (Fig. S6), nonionic surfactant tween 20 (abbreviated to Tw 20 hereafter) was finally selected. Composite films were prepared at 1 wt% of PNPs and 0.1 wt% of Tw 20 (for details, see experimental section). From the images of the films shown in Fig. 7 a, one can see that the macroscopic phase separation has been significantly suppressed and the films have smooth surfaces. UV–vis absorption measurements showed that at a wavelength of 532 nm, the films have sufficient transmittance, with the lowest transmittance of the film containing PNPs-3 over 72 % (Fig. 7b), which is sufficient for subsequent optical limiting applications.

Fig. 7c gives results from open-aperture Z-scan at a laser energy of ∼0.6 μJ for the three composite films. In all the cases, the normalized transmittance (T) exhibits a valley around Z = 0, indicating a reverse saturable absorption (RSA) behavior. It was found that the film containing PNPs-3 has a much lower transmittance (∼23 %) than that of the film containing PNPs-1 (∼85 %) and PNPs-2 (∼73 %). To quantitatively compare the NLO performance of the three films, the thickness of each film was determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations (Fig. S7). The third-order nonlinear absorption coefficient (β) at varying applied laser energies are given in Fig. 7d. The β value for the film containing PNPs-3 at a laser energy of 0.6 μJ was determined to be 2.40 × 10−6 m W−1, which is about sixteen and seven times that of the film containing PNPs-1 (1.5 × 10−7 m W−1) and PNPs-2 (3.2 × 10−7 m W−1), respectively. The better performance of the film containing PNPs-3 should be ascribed to the higher content and the larger size of the π-conjugated moieties in PNPs-3, which is known to be advantageous for NLO [57]. In addition, the optimized λ ex of PNPs-3 to get the maximum emission (540 nm) is the closest to the wavelength of the laser adopted in current study (532 nm), which means that under the same experimental condition PNPs-3 were easier to be excited. Our results confirmed that the mechanism of the NLO performance of the PNPs mainly originated from nonlinear absorption. Further, the RSA behavior observed in the three composite films originates from a sequential two-photon absorption (2 PA) process. Calculations revealed that all three samples meet the criteria for both one-photon absorption (hν>Eg) and two-photon absorption (hν>Eg/2), suggesting that the nonlinearity seen is likely due to excited state absorption (ESA) [58]. We can further analyze the role of ESA in sequential 2 PA through linear absorption patterns. UV–vis absorption studies show significant absorption across the visible spectra in the polymeric nanoparticles. Thus, when irradiated with a 532 nm (2.33 eV) laser, electrons in the ground state (E0) are excited to the first excited real state (E1), followed by ESA leading to a transition to a higher excited state E2 (virtual, 4.6 eV). Due to its virtual nature, electrons do not remain in this state, instead, they transition to the nearest available electronic state E3 in the UV region (PNPs-1: 312 nm, 3.97 eV; PNPs-2: 288 nm, 4.30 eV; PNPs-3: 280 nm, 4.42 eV and 350 nm, 3.54 eV) through phonon-assisted non-radiative processes. To explore the nature of 2 PA, we performed intensity-dependent Z-scan measurements and plotted the 2 PA coefficient against the on-axis intensity. Fig. S8 shows that the β 2PA values decreased with I0, indicating ESA. It indicates that the observed RSA is attributed to sequential 2 PA rather than genuine 2 PA. Therefore, we propose that the three polymeric nanoparticles exhibit a mechanism involving sequential 2 PA, specifically one-photon absorption followed by ESA [59,60].

As noticed in films containing CDs [45,47,61] and organic molecules [34,62], the value of β increased with decreasing laser energy. In current study, the highest β value was obtained for the film containing PNPs-3 at 0.1 μJ, which is 4.2 × 10−6 m W−1. This value is comparable to that obtained for films of alkylated CDs [47], and higher than that of luminescent carbon nanoparticles (Table S2) [63]. The optical limiting properties of the film containing PNPs-3 were further evaluated as a function of input intensity. As shown in Fig. 7e, the optical limiting threshold, which is defined as the input fluence at which the transmission is reduced to half of its original value, was determined to be 1.2 × 10−3 J cm−2. In comparison to PNPs-1 and PNPs-2, the PNPs-3 materials display a higher 2 PA coefficient and consequently a lower onset optical limiting threshold. This is primarily attributed to the strong ESA mechanism shown by the sample, which is due to the higher absorption cross section of the excited state of the π-conjugated structure compared to the ground state [64]. The greater extent of conjugation in PNPs-3 facilitates rapid energy/electron transfer, leading to enhanced nonlinearity.

In summary, we have shown that by using aldehydes bearing π-conjugated units as precursors, polymeric nanoparticles could be facilely obtained through one-step pyrolysis. These nanoparticles are soft, which undergo shape deformation on solid substrates upon drying. Partially inherit from the precursor, the nanoparticles also have large content of sp2 carbon. When the π-conjugated unit in the precursor changed from fluorene to naphthalene and further to pyrene, the content as well as the size of π-conjugated moiety in the nanoparticle increased. This structural change led to a decreased energy gap, which induced a bathochromic shift in the emission and an improved performance in the NLO response with β values comparable to those of metal-doped CDs. The results achieved in current study give deep insight on the structure-property relationship of the pyrolyzed products from π-conjugated small molecules, which could serve as a guide for the preparation of task-specific CDs or PNPs in future.
The Supporting Information is available free of charge at “××××××.”

Dandan Lu: Investigation. Jie Zhang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Mingzhi Deng: Resources, Formal analysis. Xiaoqian Liu: Formal analysis. Ziyao Liu: Methodology. Keyang Yin: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hongguang Li: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Hongguang Li reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21875129) and the assistance of Center for Structural and Property Analysis, Core Facilities Sharing Platform, Shandong University.
